title: Terraform Apply Silently Removed a Route Table Entry

context.narrative: A routine 'terraform apply' to add a new subnet to the VPC caused a 20-minute outage for an unrelated production app in the same VPC. The app lost connectivity to an on-prem database over the site-to-site VPN. The route table (rt-main) is shared by multiple subnets; one route sends on-prem-bound traffic (10.50.0.0/16) via the VPN gateway. That route is managed as a separate 'aws_route' resource, independent from the route table resource itself, which also has some routes declared inline.

artifacts.label: terraform apply output (excerpt)
artifacts.content:
~ aws_route_table.rt_main will be updated in-place
  route { } -> route { cidr_block = "10.60.0.0/16" ... } (new route added)

aws_route.on_prem_vpn: Refreshing state...
aws_route.on_prem_vpn: Destroying...

App logs: connection timeouts to on-prem DB starting immediately after the apply completed

artifacts.label: aws ec2 describe-route-tables (post-incident)
artifacts.content: Routes: [10.60.0.0/16 -> new subnet, local -> vpc, NO route for 10.50.0.0/16]

acceptable_answers: route table authoritative routes overwrote standalone route
solution.explanation: The on-prem VPN route was managed as a standalone 'aws_route' resource while the same route table was also managed with inline 'route {}' blocks in 'aws_route_table' for other routes. Terraform's AWS provider treats these as conflicting sources of truth for the table's route set; adding a new inline route caused Terraform to reconcile the table to only the inline-declared routes, destroying the standalone-managed VPN route — visible right in the apply output as 'aws_route.on_prem_vpn: Destroying...'.

context.narrative: Fix isn't just 're-add the route' — restructure so route management isn't split across two conflicting resource types. Fill in the missing inline route block so the on-prem VPN route lives inside the same aws_route_table resource as the new subnet route (and the standalone aws_route.on_prem_vpn resource should be removed entirely).

artifacts.label: corrected aws_route_table (your edit)

context.narrative: Describe the CI guardrail that would have caught this specific anti-pattern (mixing inline routes and a standalone aws_route on the same table) before the apply ever ran.

artifacts.label: current CI pipeline
artifacts.content:
on merge to main:
  - terraform plan
  - terraform apply -auto-approve
# no check for conflicting route-management resources on the same table

solution.explanation: Add a CI check (a plan-parsing script, or a Sentinel/OPA-style policy) that scans for any route table with both inline 'route' blocks and a standalone 'aws_route' resource targeting it, and fails the pipeline with an explicit error before apply — turning this known Terraform AWS footgun into a caught-in-review mistake instead of a production outage.
